Objective: To adapt peace operations to the evolving nature of conflict and move to more networked multi-dimensional approaches.
Progress per work package: The peace operations review will be finalized shortly. It has drawn on extensive consultations with Member States, missions, UN system entities, civil society and independent experts, including written submissions from 47 Member States. This process also included engagement with the General Assembly, the Security Council and relevant subsidiary organs. In the framework of the review, a task force is now developing a concept and roadmap on how to delegate the delivery of relevant programmatic tasks to entities best placed to deliver them, alongside the associated resources.

Action text / scope: Conduct a comprehensive review of the future of United Nations peace operations and present proposals for a reset for Member State consideration.
Next steps per action: The Secretary-General's peace operations review will be presented for the consideration of the General Assembly and the Security Council in June.

Objective: To build a leaner, more agile and field-oriented peace and security pillar, prioritizing prevention, advancing peacebuilding and political solutions and delivering joined-up impact.
Progress per work package: The implementation of the consolidation and streamlining of key functions and positions that were approved by Member States in A/RES/80/242 is ongoing. With regard to Action 3, DPPA and DPO have strengthened a range of mechanisms at different levels within the Departments dealing with cross-cutting functions. On Action 8, as per S/RES/2813, the Security Council has closed the mandate of UNMHA and requested the Secretary-General to prepare a plan to start the transfer of its residual functions to OSESGY. On Actions 10 and 11, coordination and mapping exercises are advancing to strengthen capacity management of UNFICYP and UNRGID. On Actions 49, 55 and 58, a criticality assessment is regularly conducted by DPPA, UNDP and DCO to determine countries where PDAs are posted, subject to funding availability.
Progress per action: Implementation of the consolidation and streamlining of key functions and positions approved by Member States in A/RES/80/242 is ongoing.
Action text / scope: Streamline and consolidate structures in DPPA and DPO.
Next steps per action: Implementation of those actions approved by A/RES/80/242 is ongoing throughout 2026. No further decision by an intergovernmental organ is envisaged.

Objective: To improve the accessibility and impact of disarmament analysis and expertise.

Consultations are ongoing, including informal conversations with Member States and the UNIDIR Board of Trustees. The co-location of disarmament centres with Regional Commissions is under examination in the context of the Regional Reset (Work Package 6).

Objective: To make UN support to Member States on drugs, crime and counter-terrorism more coherent and effective.
Progress per work package: For the proposed integration of UNICRI into UNODC, consultations are under way, including with Member States and the UNICRI Board. For Action 12, proposals have been developed to reinforce cooperation under the Global Counter-Terrorism Coordination Compact. For Action 13, a Secretary-General memorandum is being drafted on reporting lines of the Chief of the Terrorism Prevention Branch in UNODC to the Under-Secretary-General of OCT.
Progress per action: Consultations on the proposed integration of UNICRI into UNODC are under way, including with Member States and the UNICRI Board.
Action text / scope: Integrate UNICRI into UNODC while preserving its research independence, to better connect work on crime prevention, criminal justice and related areas.
Next steps per action: An initial integration proposal is expected by May, followed by further consultations, including with the UNICRI Board and Member States, through June. The integration will then be considered by CCPCJ in June and by ECOSOC in July. Further to ECOSOC consideration, the Secretary-General would submit, in the Proposed Programme Budget for 2028 regarding UNODC, the detailed arrangements for the integration, for decision by the General Assembly.

Objective: To make the humanitarian system deliver faster, more efficiently and coherently and with greater impact for people in crisis.
Progress per work package: As part of the IASC-led Humanitarian Reset, humanitarian planning timelines have been shortened by more than 50 days, and core planning documents by 67 per cent. Pilot rollouts for integrated supply chains are under way in Afghanistan, Haiti, the Occupied Palestinian Territory, Somalia and Sudan, including coordinated procurement, global logistics and country-level delivery. A common services catalogue went live in January, with 17 service lines already available. Work is also under way on humanitarian data initiatives, including the Humanitarian Data Collaborative, beneficiary data interoperability, public-private data collaboration and pooled funding for shared data priorities. The humanitarian diplomacy mechanism is already supporting coordinated analysis, messaging and engagement. To further clarify agency roles and strengthen joint work, 20 country teams have reviewed food security arrangements, joint work on mobility and displacement is moving into implementation, and discussions continue on health and nutrition.
Progress per action: Humanitarian planning timelines have been shortened by more than 50 days, and core planning documents by 67 per cent.
Action text / scope: Simplify humanitarian planning so that Needs and Response Plans are shorter, clearer and more focused on priorities and results.
Next steps per action: Simplified Humanitarian Needs and Response Plans will continue to be rolled out in 2026, with consistent application of the streamlined approach. Updated plans will be available online as they are issued. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged.

Pilot rollouts for integrated supply chains are under way in Afghanistan, Haiti, the Occupied Palestinian Territory, Somalia and Sudan, including coordinated procurement, global logistics and country-level delivery. A common services catalogue went live in January, with 17 service lines already available. Work is also under way on humanitarian data initiatives, including the Humanitarian Data Collaborative, beneficiary data interoperability, public-private data collaboration and pooled funding for shared data priorities. The humanitarian diplomacy mechanism is already supporting coordinated analysis, messaging and engagement. To further clarify agency roles and strengthen joint work, 20 country teams have reviewed food security arrangements, joint work on mobility and displacement is moving into implementation, and discussions continue on health and nutrition.
Progress per action: Joint work on mobility and displacement is moving into implementation.
Action text / scope: Clarify agency roles and strengthen joint work in food security, mobility and displacement, and health and nutrition.
Next steps per action: Alignment of agency responsibilities across food security, mobility and displacement, and health and nutrition will continue through 2026, including finalization of a Rome-based agencies collaboration blueprint, pilot-country rollout and further development of joint approaches and interoperability solutions. A Secretary-General's information brief will provide updates on progress in September. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged at this stage.

Objective: To assess the potential benefits of a merger between UNDP and UNOPS, to create a stronger engine for sustainable development, with greater scale and impact.
Progress per work package: UNDP and UNOPS have developed and submitted a joint analysis of the current state of each entity ("as-is" analysis), which has been shared with Member States. Member States have been briefed, including at the UNDP/UNOPS/UNFPA Executive Board session in February, where decision 2026/2, "Engagement with UN80 initiative", was adopted. Further analysis has been shared separately by the two entities.

Action text / scope: Conduct a thorough assessment of the potential benefits of a merger between UNDP and UNOPS.
Next steps per action: UNDP and UNOPS are currently developing a joint evidence-based assessment of the potential benefits of a merger to help determine whether a merger would strengthen the UN development system's ability to deliver results with greater scale and impact. Relevant information will be available for the Executive Board meetings in May and June. Subsequently, the Secretary-General will consider the submission of a report to the General Assembly, including proposals.

Objective: To assess the potential benefits of a merger between UNFPA and UN Women and their respective mandates to create a unified voice and platform for gender equality and women's rights.
Progress per work package: A full merger assessment report has been shared with Member States at the end of April. It is informed by engagement and consultations with Member States, the Executive Boards of UNFPA and UN Women and civil society partners. It builds on preparatory products published since March, including the current state of each entity ("as-is" analysis), preliminary findings for the assessment, and responses to frequently asked questions. The Executive Boards of UNFPA and UN Women also considered the issue in February, when decisions entitled "Engagement with UN80 initiative" were adopted.

Objective: To adapt UN country team configurations to be more impactful and efficient in support of national needs and priorities.
Progress per work package: The working group is developing principles, building blocks and recommendations for country team reconfiguration for the Secretary-General's consideration. In-depth data analysis and consultations with Member States in bilateral, regional group and plenary settings, as well as across the UN system and Resident Coordinators, have informed this process. Co-leads of other relevant work packages have also been consulted. A recalibration of DCO is underway. Member States have provided feedback on how the RC system can be strengthened. Efforts are under way to tailor RC office capacity to best meet UN country team needs.

Objective: To pool thematic knowledge across the UN development system to strengthen services for Member States.
Progress per work package: Over twenty development entities have been consulted and surveys undertaken to design the Joint Knowledge Hubs. Three pilot hubs have been selected for phase 1: Trade and Regional Integration; Productive Transformation; and Strategic Foresight. For each hub, work has started on mapping the expertise and products that the hubs will bring together, agreeing co-leadership arrangements and preparing implementation plans.

Action text / scope: Establish Joint Knowledge Hubs that bring together analysis and expertise across UN entities on key themes, providing more joined-up policy support and eliminating duplications.
Next steps per action: Implementation plans for each pilot hub will be submitted to the Secretary-General by May. The hubs will be reflected in the Secretary-General's QCPR report in May, with further details to be provided in a Secretary-General's information brief by July. The pilot phase will continue until September, with the aim of demonstrating practical results and learning lessons for the next phase. Two additional hubs, on climate change and on science, technology and innovation, are under consideration for phase 2. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged.

Objective: To make UN system expertise rapidly and easily accessible to UN country teams and Member States on demand.
Progress per work package: The design of the expertise-on-demand mechanism was endorsed by the Secretary-General in February. Since then, work has moved to the build phase, including mapping of expertise and services, design of practical arrangements, circulation of templates and development of the online catalogue.

Action text / scope: Establish an expertise-on-demand mechanism with a common catalogue of available expertise and services, clear access protocols and standardized administrative arrangements so that they can be deployed more quickly and efficiently.
Next steps per action: Work will continue on the catalogue, access protocols and standard administrative arrangements. The mechanism will be reflected in the Secretary-General's QCPR report in May, with further details to be provided in a Secretary-General's information brief by September. Initial pilots for the first UN country team will be rolled out by June with support from the Joint SDG Fund. This will be followed by refinement and system-wide operationalization by September. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged.

Objective: To sustain the UN system's action to end AIDS, sunsetting UNAIDS and mainstreaming its capacity and expertise into relevant entities of the UN development system.
Progress per work package: The first phase of transformation is under way. This has included UNAIDS Secretariat downsizing, consolidation of the UNAIDS Secretariat country footprint and identification of the 11 cosponsoring organizations as leads or affiliates. The Programme Coordinating Board (PCB) has constituted a working group that has begun its work and has consulted Member States, cosponsoring organizations and UNAIDS Secretariat.

Action text / scope: Develop and implement a transition plan to sunset the Joint UN Programme on HIV/AIDS (UNAIDS) and integrate its capacity and expertise into relevant entities of the UN development system.
Next steps per action: The PCB will hold formal multistakeholder consultations, bringing together Member States, civil society and implementing partners in May and September. An interim report of the PCB working group will be discussed by the PCB in June, with a view to preparing a final transformation plan for PCB consideration by October. The outcome would then be transmitted to ECOSOC for decision in November. Separately, specialized agencies that are UNAIDS cosponsoring organizations would be invited to take corresponding decisions regarding their participation.

Objective: To streamline environmental support to Member States for more impactful action.

Initial findings highlight fragmentation and opportunities for stronger coordination, integration and synergies to advance coherence and strengthen global environmental action at all levels.

Objective: To support and empower Resident and Humanitarian Coordinators (RC/HCs) to strengthen cross-pillar collaboration.
Progress per work package: The action plan has been finalized under joint leadership of the Deputy Secretary-General and the Emergency Relief Coordinator. Co-location of OCHA and Resident Coordinator Offices has been agreed as the standard approach for all 23 locations (43%) where this is not yet the case. The first-ever common performance-management framework for RC/HCs has been finalized. The framework for accountability between RCs and UN country teams is being revised with clearer guidance for crisis settings, alongside improved accountability arrangements for HCs. Joint transition planning has been initiated for eight countries moving out of large-scale humanitarian response, including allocations from the RC System Rapid Release Fund and additional surge capacity.

Final parts of the common performance-management system will be ready for launch in 2027. Member States will be informed of progress through a Secretary-General's information brief in May and the Secretary-General's 2026 report on QCPR implementation to ECOSOC in May.

Objective: To reduce fragmentation in UN system support services and make them more cost-effective.
Progress per work package: An outline of the Unified Services Roadmap (USR) has been completed, bringing together the prior work of the High-Level Committee on Management and the Business Innovation Group in a single framework with joint governance and public dashboards. Overall, the USR encompasses far-reaching efficiency initiatives (time-bound projects), global shared services (location-independent services) and common back offices (location-dependent services), across six service categories: procurement and supply chain, finance, ICT, logistics, human resources, and administration. The USR will reinforce horizontal enablers of programme delivery, including staff safety and security. At global level, a study to help sequence the expansion of shared services, and the implementation of the far-reaching efficiency initiatives, have also advanced. At country level, common back offices are being launched in additional countries, with "self-starter" training supporting further expansion. In parallel, the tools and methods needed to strengthen cost transparency have been developed, with country team pilots under way.

Action text / scope: Develop a Unified Services Roadmap, supported by a system-wide assessment of where sharing of global services would have the greatest impact.
Next steps per action: The study for global shared services expansion will be finalized by June. The full Unified Services Roadmap and a dashboard will be shared with Member States through a Secretary-General's information brief in June. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged at this stage.

Objective: To reduce fragmentation in UN system support services and make them more cost-effective.
Progress per work package: An outline of the Unified Services Roadmap (USR) has been completed, bringing together the prior work of the High-Level Committee on Management and the Business Innovation Group in a single framework with joint governance and public dashboards. Overall, the USR encompasses far-reaching efficiency initiatives (time-bound projects), global shared services (location-independent services) and common back offices (location-dependent services), across six service categories: procurement and supply chain, finance, ICT, logistics, human resources, and administration. The USR will reinforce horizontal enablers of programme delivery, including staff safety and security. At global level, a study to help sequence the expansion of shared services, and the implementation of the far-reaching efficiency initiatives, have also advanced. At country level, common back offices are being launched in additional countries, with "self-starter" training supporting further expansion. In parallel, the tools and methods needed to strengthen cost transparency have been developed, with country team pilots under way.

No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged at this stage.

Objective: To have the UN system make better use of shared core technology services, common technology expertise and modern tools.
Progress per work package: The first UN system-wide ICT baseline survey has been completed and opportunities for greater sharing of core services are being identified. Three initial priority use cases for the TAP have been agreed: AI toolboxes for translation and conferencing, an online platform to enable the work on expertise-on-demand (Work Package 8), and shared digital ID solutions (Work Packages 2 and 14).

Next steps per action: Building on the baseline survey results, an initial outline for sharing of core ICT services will be prepared in May. Following review, a full proposal will be presented to the Secretary-General in June. Implementation will proceed through phased expansion of shared ICT services, as part of the Unified Services Roadmap (Work Package 14), with an information brief for Member States in June.

Objective: To make publicly available UN data easier to access and use, and to build stronger joint data capacity over time.
Progress per work package: An initial Data Commons platform is being developed, formally encouraged by the Statistical Commission, with 25 entities now committed to contribute data to the next phase, as well as know-how or resources. A core delivery team and seed resources have been mobilized. A joint programme proposal is in development to sustain the shared platform and deepen progress on joint standards, production workflows, data maturity and AI readiness.

Objective: To make the UN system's training and research activities more coherent, more integrated and more useful.

Objective: To make core and pooled funding more attractive and effective.
Progress per work package: System-wide mapping and analysis have been completed across pooled funding, core funding and Funding Compact review mechanisms. This work has been supported by consultations with Member States, contributors and UN system entities. Initial findings show fragmentation across pooled funds, uneven costing and governance, structural disadvantages affecting core funding, and gaps in current Funding Compact review arrangements.
Progress per action: System-wide mapping and analysis have been completed across pooled funding. This work has been supported by consultations with Member States, contributors and UN system entities. Initial findings show fragmentation across pooled funds, uneven costing and governance.
Action text / scope: Assess pooled funding arrangements across the UN system and develop recommendations to improve the portfolio of major pooled funds.
Next steps per action: Consultations will continue through May and June, leading to a final assessment and recommendations on better pooled funding by the end of June for consideration by the Secretary-General and an information brief for Member States in July. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged at this stage.

Objective: To facilitate Member State decision-making by providing enhanced Secretariat services during mandate creation.
Progress per work package: The General Assembly adopted A/RES/80/251 in March, requesting enhanced Secretariat support in connection with mandate creation, implementation and review. Mapping of support currently being provided across mandating organs has been completed. Enhanced functionalities of the mandate registry (Work Package 29), including analytical tools, have been rolled out. Initial work is under way to improve access to information relevant to decision-making across the mandate lifecycle, including on programme budget implications of mandated tasks.

Objective: To strengthen programme and budget management by clearer assignment of mandate implementation responsibilities across the UN system and stronger internal oversight.

Terms of reference have been prepared for an assessment of ways to strengthen the internal strategic oversight and coordination of the UN system's division of labour.

Objective: To strengthen and harmonize how organizations in the UN system measure, manage and communicate their results.
Progress per work package: A concept for a practical UN system-wide mechanism for Management for Results has been developed. A draft action plan has been outlined to improve how the link between mandates, resources and results can be articulated, focused on a select number of agreed system-wide results. Consultations with UN system entities and Member States are under way to test the concept, practical options and overall viability of the proposed mechanism.

Next steps per action: The action plan will be finalized for consideration by the Secretary-General by June, in implementation of resolution A/RES/80/251. A Secretary-General's information brief is planned for July. No decision of an intergovernmental organ is envisaged at this stage.

Objective: To make mandate information and related tools easier for Member States to find and use.
Progress per work package: The pilot version of mandates.un.org was launched in October. It gives Member States a clearer view of the nearly 4,000 mandate sources cited by UN entities in support of their Proposed Programme Budget for 2026. Since then, new features have been added, including tools to compare successive versions of resolutions. A timeline has been set for further improvements. More than 100 entities have reviewed the citations for their Proposed Programme Plans for 2027, leading to significant reductions in citations. Work is also under way on additional digital tools, in line with the requests by Member States in A/RES/80/251.

Objective: To make UN Secretariat administrative services more efficient and effective by reducing duplication and consolidating services.
Progress per work package: In 2026, as approved in A/RES/80/242, Common Administrative Platforms (CAPs) were launched in New York and Bangkok to consolidate previously fragmented work. A global payroll processing team was also established in New York, Nairobi and Entebbe to unify this service. Analytical work has been initiated for other administrative service improvements.
